good_patterns.name: Vectorized operations
good_patterns.description: Use vectorized operations instead of loops for numerical computation to improve performance.

examples.bad: for i in range(len(df['col'])): df['col'][i] = df['col'][i] * 2

good_patterns.rationale: Vectorized operations in pandas and numpy are faster and more readable.

good_patterns.name: Use of scikit-learn Pipelines
good_patterns.description: Combine preprocessing and model training into a pipeline.

examples.bad: scaler = StandardScaler(); X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train); model.fit(X_train, y_train)
examples.good: pipeline = Pipeline([('scaler', StandardScaler()), ('model', LogisticRegression())]); pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)

good_patterns.rationale: Pipelines prevent data leakage and improve reproducibility.

good_patterns.name: Proper train/test split
good_patterns.description: Always split your data into training and testing sets before model training.

examples.good: X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2); model.fit(X_train, y_train); predictions = model.predict(X_test)

good_patterns.rationale: Prevents overfitting and ensures model evaluation is realistic.

anti_patterns.name: Hardcoding file paths
anti_patterns.description: Avoid using hardcoded file paths in scripts.
anti_patterns.rationale: Improves portability and reusability of code.

anti_patterns.name: Ignoring missing values
anti_patterns.description: Do not ignore missing values; handle them appropriately.
anti_patterns.rationale: Ignoring missing data can cause model errors or bias.

anti_patterns.name: Using loops for dataframe operations
anti_patterns.description: Avoid row-wise operations in dataframes using loops.

examples.bad: for i in range(len(df)): df['col'][i] = df['col'][i] * 2

anti_patterns.rationale: Loops are slower; vectorized operations are preferred.
